meta.name: PSQI Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index
meta.description: The simplified PSQI self-report contains 9 core items assessing sleep quality over the past month, covering subjective sleep quality, sleep latency, sleep duration, sleep efficiency, sleep disturbances, use of sleep medication, and daytime dysfunction.
meta.author: Buysse DJ, et al. (1989)
meta.reference: Buysse DJ, et al. The Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index: a new instrument for psychiatric practice and research. Psychiatry Res. 1989;28(2):193-213.
meta.instruction: The following questions relate to your usual sleep habits during the past month only. Please select the most appropriate response for each question.

questions.text: During the past month, how would you rate your overall sleep quality?
During the past month, how long (in minutes) has it usually taken you to fall asleep each night?
During the past month, how many hours of actual sleep did you get at night?
During the past month, how often have you had trouble sleeping because you wake up in the middle of the night or early morning?
During the past month, how often have you had trouble sleeping because you cannot breathe comfortably?
During the past month, how often have you had trouble sleeping because you have pain?
During the past month, how often have you taken medicine to help you sleep?
During the past month, how often have you had trouble staying awake while driving, eating meals, or engaging in social activity?
During the past month, how much of a problem has it been for you to keep up enough enthusiasm to get things done?

ranges.label: Good Sleep Quality
ranges.description: Your sleep quality is good with no significant sleep problems.
suggestions: Continue maintaining good sleep hygiene habits

ranges.label: Fair Sleep Quality
ranges.description: You have mild sleep difficulties. Consider improving sleep hygiene.
suggestions: Establish a regular bedtime and wake-up schedule

ranges.label: Poor Sleep Quality
ranges.description: You have moderate sleep problems that may affect daytime functioning.
suggestions: Learn relaxation techniques such as progressive muscle relaxation
Optimize your sleep environment (temperature, light, noise)

ranges.label: Very Poor Sleep Quality
ranges.description: You have severe sleep problems. Seek professional help promptly.
suggestions: Seek a medical sleep evaluation as soon as possible
Consider Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I)
Rule out possible sleep disorders such as sleep apnea
